researchFindings.claim: A refrigerator keeps food safe for up to 4 hours during a power outage — keep the door closed as much as possible
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Keep Your Food Safe During Emergencies: Power Outages, Floods & Fires
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service (FSIS)

researchFindings.claim: A full freezer keeps food safe for 48 hours; a half-full freezer keeps food safe for 24 hours — keep the door closed
Discard refrigerated perishables — raw or cooked meat, poultry, fish, eggs, and leftovers — that have been held above 40°F for more than 2 hours
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Keep Your Food Safe During Emergencies: Power Outages, Floods & Fires
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service (FSIS)

researchFindings.claim: If the outage may exceed 4 hours, transfer refrigerated perishables to a cooler with ice or frozen gel packs and maintain 40°F or below
Freezer food can be safely refrozen if it still contains ice crystals or is at 40°F or below; if fully thawed above 40°F, discard meat, poultry, and seafood
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Keep Your Food Safe During Emergencies: Power Outages, Floods & Fires
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service (FSIS)

researchFindings.claim: Never taste food to determine its safety — bacteria that cause food poisoning are odorless, colorless, and tasteless; 'when in doubt, throw it out'
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Keep Your Food Safe During Emergencies: Power Outages, Floods & Fires
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service (FSIS)
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Critical safety rule — most important whatNotToDo

researchFindings.claim: Opened condiments (mayonnaise, tartar sauce, horseradish) should be discarded if held above 50°F for more than 8 hours
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Keep Your Food Safe During Emergencies: Power Outages, Floods & Fires
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service (FSIS)

researchFindings.claim: Bread, salad greens, or any ready-to-eat foods contaminated by dripped raw meat, poultry, or fish juices must be discarded regardless of temperature history
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Keep Your Food Safe During Emergencies: Power Outages, Floods & Fires
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service (FSIS)

researchFindings.claim: Keep an appliance thermometer in your refrigerator and freezer so you can quickly verify whether food stayed at safe temperatures during a power outage
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Food and Water Safety During Power Outages and Floods

researchFindings.claim: Freeze water in containers ahead of time to provide thermal mass; this ice can also serve as drinking water if normal water supply is contaminated
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency
researchFindings.sourceOrganization: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)

recommendation: Write an action_card for the Food Safety During Power Outages parentTopic. The cluster already has detailed scenario and reference guides on specific food types; this action card fills the gap of immediate triage steps when power first goes out. Core steps: (1) keep doors closed, (2) track the 4-hour / 24-48-hour windows, (3) transfer to cooler if extended outage expected, (4) discard decision rules — perishables above 40°F for 2+ hours, (5) refreezing decision rule, (6) never taste to test, (7) when in doubt throw it out. All content sourced by USDA FSIS, FoodSafety.gov, FDA, and CDC.
